originalTitle: Cold Court’s debut EP is an infectious, glitchy genre mashup
summary: Cold Court is a brother-sister duo from Philly that seems to love nothing more than shoving all of their influences together in a messy soup that at least superficially resembles the hyperpop you've come to expect from acts like 100 Gecs. But, where songs like "Dumbest Girl Alive" goofily wink at pop punk and emo, […]
content: Cold Court is a brother-sister duo from Philly that seems to love nothing more than shoving all of their influences together in a messy soup that at least superficially resembles the hyperpop you’ve come to expect from acts like 100 Gecs. But, where songs like “Dumbest Girl Alive” goofily wink at pop punk and emo, Cold Court are a bit more self-serious, and that’s not necessarily a bad thing.  The opening track on the band’s debut EP \ (^_^) / (aka Hands Up), “Nina”, starts off sounding not unlike the dance punk bands that stormed the scene in the mid aughts like Franz Ferdinand or Test Icicles. But that all starts to change about a minute in, when the skuzzy riff gets chopped up and fed through a beat repeater. Another minute later, there’s a mellow proggy bridge that calls to mind Mars Volta. Then the whole thing ends on a barrage of glitches and digital chaos. The record largely continues in this fashion. The songs on Hands Up clearly started life on drums and guitar. But then Mini and Jojo fed their creations to a computer, added layers, rearranged the pieces, and piled on the effects. Single “Burn” is perhaps the best example of all the parts coming together. It features big rock riffs, Daft Punk-esque synths, dubstep chops, autotuned vocals, and even a rapped bridge. Yet the whole thing feels like a cohesive, seething whole as they shout, “I just want to see it burn, give a fuck about your word.” [Media: https://youtu.be/1elbrTOsUQg] They’re not the deepest lyrics, but it works. While Cold Court is clearly an exercise in maximalism, not every song goes quite as big as “Burn.” “Cola” moves more slowly, strips back some of the layers, but doesn’t turn the volume down. “Glass” almost becomes math rock as its guitars get chopped up and spit back out, and the EP’s closer “Light” is blown-out, sparkly prog. Over the course of a full album, the relentless barrage might grow exhausting. But at just 21 minutes, Hands Up doesn’t overstay its welcome, and it will be interesting to see how the band evolves as the young duo grow. Cold Court’s \ (^_^) / is available on most major streaming platforms, including Spotify, YouTube Music, Apple Music, Qobuz, and Deezer.
chineseTitle: Cold Court 的首张 EP 是一部极具感染力、充满瑕疵的流派混搭作品
chineseSummary: Cold Court 是一对来自费城的兄妹二人组，他们似乎最喜欢将他们所有的影响力塞进一碗凌乱的汤里，至少表面上类似于你所期待的 100 Gecs 等乐队的超级流行音乐。但是，像“Dumbest Girl Alive”这样的歌曲愚蠢地向流行朋克和情绪摇滚眨眼，[…]
chineseContent: Cold Court 是一对来自费城的兄妹二人组，他们似乎最喜欢将他们所有的影响力塞进一碗混乱的汤里，至少表面上类似于你所期待的 100 Gecs 等乐队的超级流行音乐。但是，像《Dumbest Girl Alive》这样的歌曲愚蠢地向流行朋克和情绪摇滚眨眼，而《Cold Court》则更加严肃一些，这不一定是坏事。  乐队首张 EP \(^_^)/（又名 Hands Up）的开场曲“Nina”，一开始听起来与弗朗兹·费迪南德 (Franz Ferdinand) 或 Test Icicles 等在中期席卷舞台的舞曲朋克乐队没什么不同。但大约一分钟后，当模糊的即兴重复段被切碎并通过节拍重复器馈送时，一切都开始发生变化。又一分钟后，有一首柔和的前卫桥让人想起火星沃尔特。然后整个事情就以一系列的故障和数字混乱而结束。该记录基本上以这种方式继续下去。 《Hands Up》中的歌曲显然是从鼓和吉他开始的。但随后 Mini 和 Jojo 将他们的创作输入计算机，添加图层，重新排列各个部分，并叠加效果。单个“Burn”也许是所有部分组合在一起的最好例子。它具有宏大的摇滚即兴重复段、愚蠢朋克式的合成器、回响贝斯节奏、自动调音的人声，甚至还有说唱桥段。然而，当他们喊道：“我只是想看到它燃烧起来，管你的话呢。”时，整件事感觉就像一个有凝聚力、沸腾的整体。 [媒体：https://youtu.be/1elbrTOsUQg]它们不是最深刻的歌词，但它有效。虽然 Cold Court 显然是一种极繁主义的实践，但并不是每首歌都像《Burn》那么大。 “可乐”移动得更慢，剥离了一些层，但没有降低音量。 《Glass》几乎变成了数学摇滚，因为它的吉他被切碎并吐出来，而 EP 的
